title: The Greed & Fear Tango: The Markets in April 2025!
gist: Damodaran's monthly market retrospective shows April 2025 equities round-tripping despite a tariff-driven $9 trillion two-day selloff, with growth, tech and momentum reasserting leadership even as rising US sovereign CDS spreads, a weakening dollar, and elevated high-yield spreads signal eroding trust in US institutions beneath the calm headline numbers.

themes.theme: Tariff Shock and V-Shaped Equity Recovery

insights.claim: April 2025 opened with a tariff-driven selloff that erased over $9 trillion in global equity market cap in two days, yet the S&P 500, NASDAQ and MSCI World Index all ended the month within 1% of where they started.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors who reacted to headline tariff news alone would have mispredicted the month's actual outcome; index-level calm masked violent intra-month round trips.
insights.quote: knocked more than $9 trillion in market cap for global equities in the next two days
insights.locator: opening paragraph, trip to Latin America

insights.claim: Regional equity performance in April was narrow-banded despite the turmoil: China was the worst performer at -3.69% in dollar terms, while India and Latin America tied for best at +3.57%, and local-currency returns were worse than dollar returns in markets like the EU, where the euro gained about 5%.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Dollar weakness flattered US-dollar-denominated returns for non-US investors, so local-currency positioning mattered more than usual in April.
insights.quote: China being the worst performing region, in terms of percentage change in dollar value, down 3.69%, and India and Latin America tied for best performing, up 3.57%
insights.locator: regional breakdown of market cap change

themes.theme: Growth, Tech and Momentum Reassert Themselves

insights.claim: US technology stocks, 29.4% of US market cap at the start of 2025, had already lost $2.34 trillion (13.19%) in Q1 2025 and fell a further $1.78 trillion in early April, but recovered to finish the month up 1.67% as the second-best performing sector, suggesting the 'obituaries' written for tech investing after Q1 were premature.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The tech-underperformance narrative from Q1 2025 was overstated; sector weight in indices barely moved despite the drawdown-and-recovery round trip.
insights.quote: technology ended the month as the second best performing sector, up 1.67% for the month, and in spite of the handwringing about their poor performance, their share of the market cap pie has barely changed
insights.locator: focus on technology at start of month

insights.claim: High-P/E (growth) US stocks, which had lost 10.9% of their value in Q1 2025, reversed to gain 1.74% in April while low-P/E (value) stocks fell 2.22%, and larger-cap companies outperformed small caps, reviving the growth/large-cap leadership pattern that had briefly broken down in Q1.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The Q1 2025 rotation into value and small-caps looks like a headfake rather than a durable regime change.
insights.quote: high PE stocks had a bad start to the year, losing 10.9% of their value in the first quarter, they made a comeback in April, up 1.74% for the year, while low PE stocks were down 2.22% for the month
insights.locator: growth versus value by earnings-to-price deciles

insights.claim: Momentum reasserted itself sharply in April: 2024's best-performing stocks rose 0.76% during the month while 2024's worst performers fell 5.31%, indicating April was a reversion to the prior momentum regime rather than a correction of 2024's 'overreach.'
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Momentum, growth and tech moved together in April, signaling the multi-year winning trade may be reasserting itself rather than genuinely mean-reverting.
insights.quote: momentum returned in force in April, with the best performing stocks in 2024 up 0.76% during the month, while the worst performing stocks of 2024 were down 5.31% for the month

insights.claim: The 'Mag Seven' stocks, combining large size, high growth and technology exposure, lost $2.6 trillion in market cap year-to-date but recovered almost all of the $1.55 trillion lost in the first week of April, ending the month roughly flat versus its start.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Watch whether this three-week reversal persists into Q2 earnings, since it would confirm 2024's leadership regime is back in force rather than genuinely broken.
insights.quote: The Mag Seven have had a bad year to date, losing $2.6 trillion in market capitalization, but they made a comeback from the depths to finish April at about the same market cap that they had at the start of the month

themes.theme: Cracks Beneath a Calm Treasury Market

insights.claim: Unlike prior crises where flight-to-safety pushed treasury yields down, US treasury rates stayed roughly flat through April's equity turmoil, and the yield curve turned U-shaped, with 3-month and 2-year rates above 5-year rates before rising again at 10- and 30-year maturities, which Damodaran partly attributes to near-term inflation expectations.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Treasuries did not perform their usual safe-haven role in April, suggesting investors are no longer certain bonds will hedge equity risk in a tariff-driven shock.
insights.quote: The yield curve, downward sloping for much of the last two years, is now u-shaped, with 3-month rates and 2-year rates higher than 5-year rates, before reverting back to higher longer term (10-year and 30-year rates).

insights.claim: The US sovereign CDS spread, the cost of insuring against US government default, rose about 38% during April, with most of the increase concentrated in the last three weeks, coinciding with (and outlasting) the threat to fire Fed Chair Jerome Powell.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A sustained rise in US CDS spreads implies the 'riskfree' US treasury rate may need a default-spread haircut going forward, a foundational valuation input worth re-examining.
insights.quote: The sovereign CDS spread for the US has risen about 38% during the course of this month, and the interesting part is that much of that rise happened in the last three weeks of the month

insights.claim: Damodaran signals he may revisit his long-standing valuation practice of using the US Treasury rate as the riskfree rate, instead netting out a default spread for the US given the rising sovereign CDS spread, a methodological shift that would raise implied discount rates across his equity valuations.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: If a widely-followed valuation authority starts stripping a US default spread out of the 'riskfree' rate, it raises implied discount rates and lowers intrinsic values across US equity valuation models.

themes.theme: Dollar Weakness and Commodity Signals on Global Growth

insights.claim: The US dollar extended its Q1 decline, falling about 3% against a broad currency basket and more than 5% against the euro in April, coinciding with the rise in US sovereign CDS spreads.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Dollar weakness alongside rising sovereign CDS spreads is a combination worth tracking as a barometer of confidence in US assets, independent of equity price action.
insights.quote: The dollar continued its decline in April, down about 3% against a broad basket of currencies, and more than 5% against the Euro.

insights.claim: Oil and broad commodity prices fell alongside equities in early April but, unlike equities, stayed down even as stocks recovered later in the month, a divergence Damodaran reads as an early signal that tariffs will slow global economic growth and commodity demand.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Commodities decoupling from the equity recovery is a leading indicator that the real economy may weaken even though markets have moved on, worth watching for confirmation in coming months.
insights.quote: in the last part of the month, they delinked, and stayed down, even as stock prices bounced back up

themes.theme: Gold and Bitcoin as Trust Barometers, Not Crisis Hedges

insights.claim: Gold rose about 5.3% in April, touching roughly $3,500, while bitcoin outperformed it, rising 14.12%; but because equity and bond markets recovered by month-end, neither asset was truly tested as a crisis hedge, and their gains instead reflect the same loss of trust visible in the CDS spread and dollar decline.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Don't read April's gold/bitcoin rally as proof of their crisis-hedge credentials; the real stress test would have required equities to stay down, which they didn't.
insights.quote: Gold had a good month in April, up about 5.3%, and hitting $3.500 towards the end of the month, but Bitcoin did even better rising almost 14.12% during the course of the month.

insights.claim: In April's cross-asset correlation data, gold's price moves tracked equities more closely than bitcoin's did, undercutting the narrative of bitcoin as 'digital gold' during this particular episode.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Correlation regimes between gold and bitcoin shift over time; treat the 'digital gold' framing as time-varying rather than structural.
insights.quote: gold prices movements were more closely tied to stock price movements, at least during the month

themes.theme: Pricing the Risk: ERP, VIX and Credit Spreads

insights.claim: The S&P 500 equity risk premium spiked above 5% in early April before drifting back down to 4.58% by month-end, almost exactly matching its start-of-month level of 4.59%, meaning the market's forward-looking price of equity risk round-tripped despite the intra-month chaos.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: ERP is Damodaran's preferred real-time gauge of market fear/greed; its round trip confirms April was a repricing scare rather than a durable regime shift in risk appetite.
insights.quote: the equity risk premium decreased in fits and starts over the rest of the month to end at almost the same value (4.58%) as at the start of the month (4.59%)

insights.claim: The VIX spiked to 52.33 on April 8 before falling back to 24.70 by month-end, close to its starting level of 22.28, a pattern mirroring the equity risk premium's round trip.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A VIX spike above 50 followed by a full retracement within three weeks is historically rare and signals unusually fast market absorption of shock news.
insights.quote: the VIX soared in the first few days of the month to peak at 52.33 on April 8, and then decreased over the rest of the month to a level (24.70) close to where it was at the start of the month (22.28)
insights.locator: VIX discussion alongside equity risk premium

insights.claim: Unlike the equity risk premium and VIX, high-yield bond default spreads rose more than 1% in the first week of April and, despite partially declining afterward, ended the month higher than where they started.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A divergence between calm equity risk pricing and still-elevated credit spreads is a classic tell to watch, since credit often leads equities in confirming or denying a growth scare.
insights.quote: the bond default spreads did end the month at levels higher than at the start, indicating at least at this point that near term concerns about the economy and the ensuing default risk have not subsided
insights.locator: bond market price of risk discussion

insights.claim: Cross-asset correlation analysis for April showed stocks and treasury bonds moving together for much of the month, atypical crisis behavior since bonds normally rally as stocks fall, reinforcing that April was 'asset repricing' rather than classic panic-driven flight to safety.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The breakdown of the normal stock-bond negative correlation weakens the traditional 60/40 diversification benefit during tariff-driven shocks specifically.
insights.quote: stock and treasury bond prices moved together much of the month, not something that you would expect during a crisis, when bond prices gain as stock prices fall

themes.theme: Lessons on Market Behavior and Policy Sensitivity

insights.claim: Damodaran argues markets have shown unusual resilience over the past five years partly because market influence has become diffuse, with social media and alternative information sources supplementing or replacing traditional gatekeepers like the financial press and large portfolio managers, reducing herd-driven overreaction.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Reduced concentration of market-moving influence among a few large institutional voices may structurally dampen future panic-driven selloffs relative to historical crises.
insights.quote: market influence has become diffuse, with social media and alternative sources of information supplementing and often replacing the traditional influencers

insights.claim: April's rebound was driven largely by the US administration reversing course, pausing the 'liberation day' tariffs and quickly withdrawing the threat to remove Fed Chair Powell, showing that even an administration otherwise unresponsive to press criticism or foreign pushback will bend to sustained market selling pressure.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Sustained equity selloffs appear to be a more effective constraint on tariff and Fed-independence risk than political or diplomatic pressure, useful for gauging how far future policy shocks might go before reversal.
insights.quote: an administration that has been impervious to Wall Street journal editorials, warnings from economists and counter threats from other governments has been willing to bend to market selling pressure

insights.claim: Damodaran contends that portfolio damage in April was likely proportional to how much time investors spent consuming market-timing commentary from CNBC and pundits during the volatility, arguing against the case that active or tactical management protected investors better than staying the course.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Reinforces a passive, buy-and-hold discipline argument: investors who tactically traded April's volatility on expert commentary likely underperformed a do-nothing approach.
insights.quote: the extent of damage that April did to investor portfolios was directly proportional to how much time they spent watching CNBC and listening to (or reading) what market experts told them to do

insights.claim: Damodaran used April's mid-month selloff to buy BYD when its price dropped below his $80 limit order, while Palantir and Mercado Libre, his two other pre-set limit buys, approached but did not breach his target prices, so he did not add to those positions.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence of a limit-price contrarian-investing framework in action; BYD entered his portfolio near $80, a reference valuation anchor.
insights.quote: I did use the mid-month sell off to add BYD, a stock that I like, to my portfolio, when its price dipped below my limit price ($80).

insights.claim: Damodaran still holds six of the seven 'Mag Seven' stocks, having previously exited Tesla entirely and trimmed Nvidia to a quarter of his original position, and plans to revisit those valuations as Q1 earnings are reported.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A valuation-driven investor has already de-risked out of Tesla and cut Nvidia exposure sharply ahead of this earnings season, a data point on how mega-cap tech valuations look to a skeptical practitioner.
insights.quote: six of the seven Mag Seven stocks that remain in my portfolio (Tesla is out of my portfolio and Nvidia is at a quarter of my original holding)
